scenarios.name: Coffee Shop
scenarios.description: Order coffee and cafe drinks easily
scenarios.tip: Use this for cafe, bakery, and takeaway-counter moments; simple drink, sugar, and takeaway language matters more than heavy customization.

scenarios.name: Street Food / Restaurant
scenarios.tip: Pointing still helps, but short phrases for one portion, bottled water, takeaway, and allergy-sensitive clarification do more work than long ordering sentences.

scenarios.name: Ride App / Taxi
scenarios.tip: Showing the address on your phone helps, but the bigger Germany need is clear stop-here, station, and card-or-cash clarification.

scenarios.name: Asking the Price
scenarios.tip: Use this mostly for price confirmation, item selection, and receipt clarity; bargaining is low priority for Germany.

scenarios.name: Polite Basics
scenarios.tip: A brief greeting, thanks, and excuse-me phrase cover a large share of everyday Germany travel friction, especially with strangers and staff.

scenarios.name: Convenience Store
scenarios.tip: Keep this scenario practical for station kiosks, mini-marts, and drugstores: water, bag, card, and receipt language matters most.

scenarios.name: Hotel / Hostel
scenarios.tip: Reservation, check-in, checkout, and luggage help belong first; many front-desk interactions stay brief and formal.

scenarios.name: Directions
scenarios.tip: Save the address, station name, and platform details in your phone because transit-heavy directions often hinge on exact place names.

scenarios.name: Simple Problems
scenarios.tip: Start with repair phrases and slower-speech requests before longer explanations; screen-showing helps when fast replies pile up.

scenarios.name: Small Talk
scenarios.tip: Keep small talk light; first-visit comments and food or city compliments are enough for the first authoring wave.

notes: Shared traveler baseline scaffold for German, now reprioritized toward Germany travel utility and backed by a full 70-row source scaffold plus a ranked first-wave shortlist. Fill translation, pronunciation, and audio posture before runtime wiring.
